quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Substitution with rewriting"?

options.text: When the integrand needs algebraic manipulation: ∫x√(x+1)dx with u = x+1, x = u-1, dx = du
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀; produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve

quiz_questions.explanation: "Substitution with rewriting" is defined as: When the integrand needs algebraic manipulation: ∫x√(x+1)dx with u = x+1, x = u-1, dx = du. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Completing the square"?

options.text: L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a); the tangent line at x = a used to estimate f(x) for x near a

quiz_questions.explanation: "Completing the square" is defined as: Rewrite ax² + bx + c in the form a(x-h)² + k to enable substitution or match a known integral form. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Back-substitution"?

options.text: ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du; derived from the product rule: d(uv) = u dv + v du

After integrating in terms of u, replace u with g(x) for indefinite integrals; for definite integrals, use changed limits instead
If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither

quiz_questions.explanation: "Back-substitution" is defined as: After integrating in terms of u, replace u with g(x) for indefinite integrals; for definite integrals, use changed limits instead. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Recognizing patterns"?

options.text: ∫f'(x)/f(x)dx = ln|f(x)| + C (u = f(x)); ∫f'(x)·[f(x)]ⁿdx = [f(x)]ⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C

lim[f ± g] = lim f ± lim g; lim[f·g] = lim f · lim g; lim[f/g] = lim f / lim g (if lim g ≠ 0); lim[fⁿ] = (lim f)ⁿ

quiz_questions.explanation: "Recognizing patterns" is defined as: ∫f'(x)/f(x)dx = ln|f(x)| + C (u = f(x)); ∫f'(x)·[f(x)]ⁿdx = [f(x)]ⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques is accurate?

options.text: Key consequences include: (1) if f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant, (2) if f'(x) = g'(x) for all x, then f(x) = g(x) + C, (3) if f'(x) > 0 on (a,b), then f is strictly increasing.
Logarithmic Differentiation systematically uses properties of logarithms to simplify complex differentiation.
Derivatives represent rates of change in any context: population growth rates, velocity, temperature change rates, marginal economic quantities, and reaction rates.
U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques handles more complex substitution scenarios.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques?

options.text: Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Signal processing: complex integrals in Fourier analysis require sophisticated substitution techniques to transform between time and frequency domains
Market saturation: adoption of new technology (smartphones, streaming services) follows an S-curve as the product approaches market saturation
Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques?

options.text: Radioactive decay: integrating the decay rate dN/dt = -λN gives N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), the fundamental decay equation
Structural load analysis: EVT guarantees the existence of a maximum stress point on a continuous beam, ensuring engineers can find and design for worst-case loads
Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse
Statistical mechanics: partition function integrals in thermodynamics often require completing the square before Gaussian substitution
Temperature modeling: since temperature changes continuously, the IVT guarantees that between any two measured temperatures, every intermediate value was achieved
Population modeling: if the growth rate dP/dt = f(t) is known, integrating gives the population P(t) = ∫f(t)dt + P₀

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques?

options.text: Profit maximization: after finding the critical point of P(x), checking P''(x) < 0 confirms it's a maximum profit level, not a minimum
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
Signal processing: complex integrals in Fourier analysis require sophisticated substitution techniques to transform between time and frequency domains
Quality control: using normal distribution integrals to determine the probability that a manufactured part falls within specifications (Six Sigma methodology)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques in practice?

options.text: Ladder sliding down a wall: if the base moves away at 2 ft/s, how fast does the top slide down? Classic Pythagorean related rates problem used in construction safety
Probability: the Cauchy distribution has CDF (1/π)arctan(x) + 1/2, obtained by integrating the probability density 1/(π(1+x²))
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution
Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques.
